title: RSI / Airway
subtitle: Preoxygenation, intubation, and failed-airway setup
inKit: ETT (7.0–7.5 for most adults; 8.0 for large males) — confirm cuff inflates with 10 mL syringe
Malleable stylet — pre-set hockey-stick or gentle curve before handing to intubator
10 mL syringe for cuff inflation
Laryngoscope handle with Mac 3 and Mac 4 blades (confirm light before patient arrives)
Water-soluble or lidocaine lubricant for stylet tip and distal ETT
Colorimetric CO2 detector
Bite block or size-appropriate oral airway adjunct
Tape or commercial tube holder (Thomas or Anchor Fast)
outsideKit: Video laryngoscope (GlideScope, C-MAC, or equivalent) — at bedside BEFORE paralytic is given
BVM with PEEP valve and O2 reservoir bag — confirm PEEP valve is actually attached
Nasal cannula at 15 L/min for apneic oxygenation during laryngoscopy
Suction (Yankauer) — turned ON, tested, positioned at right side of patient's head
Waveform capnography device (continuous; colorimetric alone is inadequate)
Patent IV access confirmed with flush before any medications
RSI medications: induction agent + paralytic — drawn, labeled, dose verified aloud
Post-intubation sedation ready before paralytic given (propofol, ketamine, or fentanyl + versed infusion)
Ventilator set up: TV 6–8 mL/kg IBW, rate 14, FiO2 100%, PEEP 5
Cardiac monitor: continuous O2 sat (waveform), BP cycling every 2 min, rhythm strip
commonlyForgotten: Nasal cannula for apneic oxygenation — leave on under the BVM during laryngoscopy; extends safe apnea time
PEEP valve on BVM — most crash cart BVMs don't come pre-attached; preoxygenation is ineffective without it
Waveform capnography — colorimetric gives false positives in low-flow and cardiac arrest states
Suction ON before first bag-mask ventilation — aspiration during mask ventilation precedes tube placement
Post-intubation sedation drawn and labeled — paralytic wears off in 10–15 min; paralyzed and awake is a critical safety event
Second ETT of different size — size unavailable at the worst moment is a real event
patientSetup: Ramp position: elevate head until external auditory meatus aligns with sternal notch — critical in obese and pregnant patients; use blankets, ramp pillow, or scoop mattress
Head of bed at comfortable working height for intubator — intubator should stand, not crouch
IV access confirmed patent with flush before any medications administered
Monitoring: continuous O2 sat (waveform), waveform ETCO2, BP cycling every 2 min, cardiac telemetry
Assign roles before induction: one on BVM, one on drugs, one for cricoid if used — brief everyone aloud
Verbalize the primary and backup plan (primary airway, backup device, surgical rescue) before paralytic
sterileSetup: RSI is not a sterile procedure — clean technique with clean gloves
Avoid contaminating distal 10 cm of ETT and cuff
Mayo stand in order: ETT + stylet assembled and shaped, syringe attached, lubricant applied; laryngoscope blade clicked in and light confirmed
Backup blade (different size or video) on mayo stand or immediately reachable
Suction Yankauer uncapped and positioned at right side of patient's head before induction
backupEquipment: Bougie (Eschmann stylet) — in hand or on mayo stand; use at first difficult view, not after repeated failed attempts
Video laryngoscope — must be physically in the room before paralytic given
Supraglottic airway (LMA i-gel or King LT) — 'can't intubate, can oxygenate' bridge device
Surgical airway kit (cric tray) — must be in the room before RSI begins; CICO is the indication
Difficult airway cart location known to everyone in the room before induction begins
references: Walls RM, Murphy MF, et al. Manual of Emergency Airway Management, 5th ed. Lippincott 2018.
Brown CA 3rd, Bair AE, Pallin DJ, Walls RM. Rapid Sequence Intubation for Patients in the Emergency Department. Ann Emerg Med. 2021.
Weingart SD, Levitan RM. Preoxygenation and prevention of desaturation during emergency airway management. Ann Emerg Med. 2012;59(3):165–175.
ACEP Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Management of the Difficult Airway 2013.

title: Surgical Airway (Cric)
subtitle: Can't intubate, can't oxygenate — front-of-neck access
inKit: Scalpel (#20 or #10 blade)
Tracheal hook (cric hook) — to hold the inferior thyroid cartilage cephalad while tube passes
Curved clamp (Kelly or Rochester hemostat) — to blunt-dissect and dilate the membrane opening
6.0 cuffed ETT or 6.0 cuffed cric tube (Shiley or Portex)
10 mL syringe for cuff inflation
Suction Yankauer with suction tubing
Cloth tape or suture to secure tube
outsideKit: Suction ON at bedside — blood and secretions will obscure the cricothyroid membrane
BVM with O2 source connected and ready for immediate ventilation
Waveform capnography to confirm tube position after placement
Headlamp or procedure light — overhead room light alone is inadequate for emergency neck dissection
Second person to stabilize the larynx and maintain neutral cervical alignment in trauma
10 cc syringe pre-charged (for immediate cuff inflation) on the field
commonlyForgotten: Lighting — headlamp is critical; most ED procedure lights are overhead and cast shadows at neck level
Suction before you cut — blood and secretions fill the cric opening within seconds of skin incision
Cric hook to retract and hold the inferior thyroid cartilage cephalad before tube insertion — without it the membrane collapses
Confirm tube position with waveform capnography, not colorimetric — low-output states give false negatives
Horizontal incision through the membrane after the vertical skin incision — operators confuse the two planes under pressure
patientSetup: Extend the neck: shoulder roll under shoulders; do NOT hyperextend in trauma — maintain inline cervical stabilization
Palpate landmarks before cutting: thyroid notch → descend midline → find soft space = cricothyroid membrane
Stabilize the larynx: non-dominant thumb and middle finger on thyroid cartilage; index finger on membrane
Operator at right side of patient for right-handed operator
Mark membrane with skin marker in semi-elective settings; in emergency, use finger stabilization
Remove neck jewelry and loosen collar or cervical collar before prep
sterileSetup: Betadine or chlorhexidine prep over anterior neck if time permits — in CICO, clean gloves and proceed immediately
Sterile drape if time permits
Mayo stand setup: scalpel, cric hook, clamp, tube + syringe, suction, gauze
Suction immediately adjacent to the working field — blood clearing is essential for visualization
backupEquipment: Needle cricothyrotomy (14g angiocath): temporizing bridge for maximum 30–45 min with jet ventilation — do not delay surgical cric if jet ventilation is unavailable
Melker percutaneous cric kit (Seldinger technique): alternative if scalpel cric not within operator's trained skill set
Video laryngoscope: one final attempt may be reasonable in selected scenarios before declaring CICO
references: Walls RM, Murphy MF, et al. Manual of Emergency Airway Management, 5th ed. Lippincott 2018.
Frerk C, Mitchell VS, McNarry AF, et al. Difficult Airway Society 2015 guidelines for management of unanticipated difficult intubation in adults. Br J Anaesth. 2015;115(6):827–848.
Duggan LV, et al. Cricothyrotomy for emergency airway. N Engl J Med. 2021;385:e42.

title: Central Venous Catheter
subtitle: Triple-lumen CVC: IJ, subclavian, or femoral under ultrasound
inKit: Triple-lumen CVC (15–16 cm for IJ/subclavian; 20 cm for femoral) — Arrow, Cook, or equivalent
Introducer needle (18g, included in kit)
Guidewire (J-tip, 0.035") — keep track of depth mark at skin at all times
Vessel dilator
1% lidocaine in 5 mL syringe with 25g and 22g needles
10 mL syringe for aspiration and flushing
Sterile 4x4 gauze packs
Large sterile drapes and fenestrated drape
Chlorhexidine prep applicator sticks (×2 minimum)
Suture (3-0 silk on straight needle) or StatLock securement device
Heparin-lock caps (×3, one per lumen)
Transparent occlusive dressing (Tegaderm or Biopatch + Tegaderm)
outsideKit: Ultrasound machine with high-frequency linear probe (10–15 MHz) — CVC without ultrasound violates standard of care at most centers
Sterile ultrasound probe cover and sterile gel (not in the kit)
Sterile gown — max barrier precautions cut infection risk; this is not optional
Extra chlorhexidine prep sticks (one kit stick is not enough for adequate prep)
Cardiac monitor: continuous rhythm strip during wire passage (ectopy = wire in RV, pull back)
10 mL NS flushes ×3 (one per lumen) to prime all lumens before suturing
Chest X-ray ordered before using line for medications — confirm tip in SVC and no pneumothorax
Blood culture bottles if simultaneously obtaining blood cultures at time of placement
commonlyForgotten: Sterile probe cover — ultrasound probe must be sterile for real-time guidance; sterile gel is separate from non-sterile gel
Marking wire depth at skin before dilating — losing track of the wire is a critical event
Confirming wire in vessel on ultrasound before dilating — see the wire in the vein before the dilator passes
Priming ALL three lumens with NS flush before the dressing goes on — unprimed lumens clot immediately
Chest X-ray before first medication use — distal tip position and pneumothorax must be confirmed
patientSetup: IJ approach: Trendelenburg 15–20°, head turned contralateral to insertion site, roll under ipsilateral shoulder to open the neck
Subclavian: roll under scapulae (not mid-back), arm tucked at side, slight Trendelenburg
Femoral: leg extended and externally rotated, table flat
Time-out before draping: confirm patient ID, site, side, allergies (especially chlorhexidine), procedure
Full max barrier precautions: cap, mask with eye protection, sterile gown, sterile gloves — not a suggestion
Non-sterile assistant positioned for ultrasound machine, medications, specimen tubes, and sharps disposal
sterileSetup: Chlorhexidine prep in back-and-forth scrub motion for 30 seconds; allow FULL 2 minutes to dry — wet chlorhexidine is not effective
Betadine alternative if chlorhexidine allergy; allow 2 min to dry
Large sterile drape covering patient head to toe with fenestrated hole over site
Mayo stand setup in order of use: lidocaine syringe, introducer needle, guidewire, dilator, catheter, caps
Sterile ultrasound probe cover applied before skin puncture — do not sonicate without cover
backupEquipment: Introducer sheath (Cordis): if 8.5 Fr large-bore access needed for resuscitation, emergent RHC, or transvenous pacer passage
Dialysis catheter (Vas-Cath): if CRRT or acute hemodialysis is anticipated
Two large-bore peripheral IVs as bridge if central access is delayed
Arterial line if continuous hemodynamic monitoring is simultaneously required
references: McGee DC, Gould MK. Preventing Complications of Central Venous Catheterization. N Engl J Med. 2003;348:1123–1133.
Marschall J, et al. SHEA/IDSA Practice Recommendation: Strategies to Prevent CLABSI. Infect Control Hosp Epidemiol. 2014;35(7):753–771.
Hind D, et al. Ultrasonic locating devices for central venous cannulation: meta-analysis. BMJ. 2003;327:361.
O'Grady NP, et al. Guidelines for the Prevention of Intravascular Catheter-Related Infections. CDC/HICPAC 2011.

title: Chest Tube / Thoracostomy
subtitle: Large-bore drainage for hemothorax, pneumothorax, or empyema
inKit: Chest tube: 28–36 Fr for hemothorax; 20–24 Fr for uncomplicated pneumothorax — select size before opening
Large curved clamp (Kelly or Rochester hemostat) ×2 — one for blunt dissection, one for tube passage
Scalpel (#10 blade, or #21 for large incision)
20 mL syringe + 25g and 22g needles for lidocaine infiltration
0-silk or 0-Prolene suture on large curved needle for tube anchoring
Chlorhexidine prep applicators
Large sterile drape
Petroleum gauze (Xeroform) for occlusive dressing around tube exit site
Silk tape (Elastoplast or cloth tape) for tube anchoring
outsideKit: Pleural drainage system (Atrium Oasis, Pleur-evac, or equivalent) — filled to water-seal line with sterile water BEFORE connecting
Sterile connector tubing from chest tube to drainage system
Wall suction connected to drainage system at -20 cmH2O
Adequate lidocaine: 1–2%, at least 20–30 mL for adult infiltration (most kits include only 10 mL)
Morphine or fentanyl IV for procedural analgesia — chest tube insertion without analgesia is not acceptable
Chest X-ray ordered immediately post-procedure to confirm tube position
Pulse ox and cardiac monitor throughout procedure and recovery
Non-sterile assistant for tubing connection, drainage system, suction
commonlyForgotten: Pre-filling the drainage system with sterile water to the fill line BEFORE connecting — the water-seal doesn't function without it
Petroleum gauze occlusive dressing around the tube exit site — prevents air entrainment around the tube shaft
Adequate analgesia before incision — this is the most painful common bedside procedure in emergency medicine
Confirming suction is active and water-seal is oscillating appropriately after connection
Horizontal mattress anchor suture — tube falls out without secure anchoring; tape alone is insufficient
patientSetup: Position: ipsilateral arm raised above head (hand behind head or draped overhead) to expose lateral chest wall
Target: safe triangle — bounded by lateral pectoralis border anteriorly, mid-axillary line posteriorly, and 4th–5th ICS superiorly
Mark insertion site with pen before prepping when possible
Elevate head of bed 30–45° for alert patients to reduce venous engorgement
Systemic analgesia before prep: IV opioid 5–10 min before procedure; local anesthesia at site with rib periosteum block
sterileSetup: Chlorhexidine prep over large area: axilla to midline, 3 complete passes; allow 2 min to dry
Large sterile drape covering patient from neck to waist
Full sterile gown, gloves, mask, and eye protection
Mayo stand in order: lidocaine syringe, scalpel, clamp ×2, chest tube (clamped at distal end), suture, petroleum gauze
backupEquipment: Pigtail catheter (14–16 Fr Seldinger kit): for uncomplicated pneumothorax or free-flowing effusion when large-bore tube is not indicated
Needle decompression (14g angiocath, 2nd ICS MCL): for tension pneumothorax with hemodynamic compromise — do not wait for chest tube in arrest
CT surgery or thoracic consult: initial hemothorax >1500 mL OR ongoing >200 mL/hr
references: Dev SP, Nascimento B Jr, Simone C, Chien V. Chest-Tube Insertion. N Engl J Med. 2007;357:e15.
Mowery NT, et al. EAST Practice Management Guidelines for Management of Hemothorax and Occult Pneumothorax. J Trauma. 2011;70(2):510–518.
Laws D, Neville E, Duffy J; BTS Pleural Disease Group. BTS guidelines for the insertion of a chest drain. Thorax. 2003;58 Suppl 2:ii53–59.

title: Arterial Line
subtitle: Radial A-line: continuous hemodynamic monitoring and ABG access
inKit: 20g arterial catheter-over-needle (2-inch preferred for obese or edematous wrists) — Arrow or Acra-Cut
Guidewire (0.018" or 0.025" Seldinger wire, included in most kits)
10 mL syringe for aspiration confirmation
Transparent occlusive dressing (Tegaderm)
StatLock or 3-0 suture for secure anchoring
Chlorhexidine prep applicator
Sterile mini-drape or sterile towel
outsideKit: Pressure transducer tubing kit (Edwards, Argon, or BD) — NOT included in arterial kit; must be ordered separately
Pressure bag: 500 mL NS, inflated to 300 mmHg, tubing fully primed with no air bubbles
Pressure transducer holder (pole mount or arm mount)
Wrist roll: rolled gauze or folded towel to dorsiflex wrist 45–60°
Cloth tape to secure wrist in dorsiflexed position
Cardiac monitor with arterial waveform channel selected and scaled appropriately
Transducer zeroed to phlebostatic axis (4th ICS, mid-axillary line) BEFORE line is placed
commonlyForgotten: Wrist dorsiflexion setup BEFORE prepping — radial artery becomes 30–50% more superficial and palpable with proper wrist extension
Priming the pressure tubing and zeroing the transducer before the catheter is placed — avoids delay and air embolism risk
Suturing or StatLocking the catheter — tape alone causes dislodgement; A-line coming out during vasopressor titration is critical
Confirming arterial waveform (pulsatile, systolic > diastolic, dicrotic notch visible) before applying dressing
Allen's test in elective cases: confirm ulnar collateral flow exists before radial occlusion in a patient who cannot tolerate hand ischemia
patientSetup: Non-dominant wrist preferred in awake cooperative patients
Dorsiflex wrist 45–60° over wrist roll; tape index finger, middle finger, and ring finger back without occluding circulation
Palpate radial pulse 1–2 cm proximal to flexor wrist crease; mark with thumbnail
Position arm at or below heart level
For ultrasound guidance: linear probe (10–15 MHz) oriented transverse over radial artery
sterileSetup: Chlorhexidine prep swab over wrist; allow to dry
Mini sterile drape over wrist (included in kit or use sterile towel)
Sterile gloves
Mayo stand: catheter assembled, syringe attached, dressing open and ready
Have pressure tubing primed, zeroed, and handed over sterile field before catheter is placed
backupEquipment: Ultrasound-guided placement (linear probe with sterile cover): use when radial pulse is weak, absent, or after two failed palpation attempts
Femoral artery approach: use if bilateral radial access fails; easily compressible, but higher infection risk
Brachial artery approach: end artery — use only as last resort due to hand ischemia risk
Non-invasive NIBP cycling every 1 min as bridge while arterial access is established
references: Scheer BV, Perel A, Pfeiffer UJ. Clinical review: Complications and risk factors of peripheral arterial catheters used for hemodynamic monitoring. Crit Care. 2002;6(3):199–204.
Brzezinski M, Luisetti T, London MJ. Radial artery cannulation: a comprehensive review of recent anatomic and physiologic investigations. Anesth Analg. 2009;109(6):1763–1781.
Slogoff S, et al. Complications of radial artery catheterization. Anesth Analg. 1983;62(2):180–184.

title: Lumbar Puncture
subtitle: CSF sampling for meningitis, SAH workup, and ICP measurement
inKit: Spinal needle: 20g Quincke for standard adult; 22g or 24g Sprotte/Whitacre pencil-point to reduce post-LP headache
Stylet (always inserted before needle advances — never removed until tip is intrathecal)
CSF collection tubes ×4 — label 1, 2, 3, 4 BEFORE starting the procedure
Three-way stopcock for opening pressure measurement
Manometer (confirm included — some kits omit it)
Sterile drapes and fenestrated drape
Chlorhexidine prep applicators ×3 minimum
1% lidocaine, 10 mL syringe with 25g and 22g needles for skin and deep infiltration
Sterile gauze 4x4s and sterile bandage
outsideKit: Ultrasound (linear probe, 10–15 MHz) for interspinous space identification in obese, elderly, or scoliotic patients — significantly improves first-pass success
Fluoroscopy guidance (IR) if bedside attempts fail after appropriate attempt
Pre-procedure analgesia (acetaminophen or ibuprofen, NOT opioid) 30–60 min before to reduce post-LP headache
Lab orders placed BEFORE starting: cell count (tubes 1 and 4), glucose, protein, Gram stain/culture; xanthochromia if SAH suspected
Extra CSF tubes if cytology, TB, fungal Ag, or unusual studies needed — these need 3–8 mL additional
Non-sterile assistant to maintain patient in flexed lateral decubitus and pass specimen tubes without contaminating field
commonlyForgotten: Label all 4 tubes BEFORE opening the kit — unlabeled tubes in a busy resuscitation bay get mixed up
Measure and document opening pressure BEFORE removing any CSF — closing pressure also documented
Replace stylet before withdrawing the needle — reduces post-LP headache and prevents intraspinal epidermoid formation
Send tube 4 for cell count in addition to tube 1: RBC count that decreases from tube 1 to tube 4 supports traumatic tap over true pleocytosis
Chlorhexidine must be fully dry before spinal needle is inserted — wet chlorhexidine is directly neurotoxic
patientSetup: Lateral decubitus (fetal position): knees to chest, chin tucked, back arched toward operator; pillow between knees maintains spine horizontal
OR seated: patient leaning forward over pillow on bedside table, arms crossed and supported — better for obese patients to flex spine
Target L3–L4 or L4–L5 interspace: iliac crest level = approximately L4 spinous process
Palpate and mark the interspace with thumbnail indent before prepping
Operator seated for lateral approach; bring table to comfortable working height
Non-sterile assistant stabilizes patient in flexed position — patient movement during needle passage is the most common cause of blood contamination
sterileSetup: Chlorhexidine prep in widening circular motion ×3 passes; allow full 2 minutes to dry — mandatory before spinal needle insertion
Sterile drapes with fenestrated hole over target interspace
Sterile gloves and mask
Lay out in order of use: lidocaine syringe, exploring needle, spinal needle, stopcock + manometer, collection tubes
Bevel of spinal needle oriented parallel to dural fibers (bevel facing patient's flank in lateral position) to reduce headache
backupEquipment: Ultrasound-guided approach: use when bony landmarks are not palpable; mark interspace with probe before prepping
Fluoroscopy-guided LP (IR): for severe obesity, scoliosis, prior lumbar surgery, or multiple failed bedside attempts
24g atraumatic needle (Sprotte): higher first-pass difficulty but dramatically lower post-LP headache incidence in appropriate patients
Blood patch (anesthesia consult): for refractory post-LP headache that does not resolve with caffeine and supine positioning
references: Straus SE, Thorpe KE, Holroyd-Leduc J. How do I perform a lumbar puncture and analyze the results to diagnose bacterial meningitis? JAMA. 2006;296(16):2012–2022.
van Crevel H, Hijdra A, de Gans J. Lumbar puncture and the risk of herniation: when should we first perform CT? J Neurol. 2002;249(2):129–137.
Armon C, Evans RW; Therapeutics and Technology Assessment Subcommittee. Addendum to assessment: prevention of post-lumbar puncture headaches. Neurology. 2005;65(4):510–512.

title: Thoracentesis
subtitle: Pleural fluid sampling or therapeutic drainage by ultrasound guidance
inKit: Thoracentesis catheter-over-needle assembly (Unipleur, Arrow, or Thal-Quick) — 8–9 cm with side holes
Collection tubing with three-way stopcock
Large drainage bag (1000–1500 mL) or vacuum collection bottles ×2
Sterile drapes and fenestrated drape
Chlorhexidine prep applicators ×3
1% lidocaine 10 mL syringe with 25g and 22g needles — infiltrate generously to parietal pleura
10 mL and 20 mL sterile syringes for aspiration
Sterile specimen cups ×3
Sterile 4x4 gauze packs and adhesive bandage
outsideKit: Ultrasound machine with low-frequency probe (3.5–5 MHz curvilinear) — mark effusion IMMEDIATELY before procedure; do NOT mark hours earlier with position changes
Sterile ultrasound probe cover for real-time guidance during needle insertion
Lab tubes for fluid analysis — order BEFORE starting: LDH, protein, glucose, cell count, Gram stain/culture, cytology
Heparinized syringe for pleural fluid pH if parapneumonic effusion suspected
Chest X-ray or bedside ultrasound immediately post-procedure to rule out pneumothorax
Pulse ox and cardiac monitor throughout procedure and 1 hour after
Non-sterile assistant to hold patient in seated position and pass specimen containers
commonlyForgotten: Re-marking the insertion site with ultrasound immediately before procedure — the effusion border shifts with position changes
Volume limit: remove no more than 1000–1500 mL at one sitting — re-expansion pulmonary edema risk with larger volumes even in experienced hands
Inserting needle ABOVE the rib, not below — neurovascular bundle (artery, vein, nerve) runs along the inferior rib margin
Pleural fluid pH in suspected parapneumonic effusion — pH below 7.20 indicates the effusion requires tube drainage, not just sampling
Bevel facing down (away from lung parenchyma) during aspiration to reduce pneumothorax risk
patientSetup: Seated position: patient upright and leaning forward onto pillow on overbed table — opens posterior intercostal spaces
Arms crossed in front and supported to keep shoulder blades retracted
Target: posterior approach, 2 interspaces below the top of effusion on ultrasound, above 9th rib to avoid diaphragm
Mark site with skin marker immediately after ultrasound scan; indent skin with thumbnail as second reference
If patient cannot sit upright: lateral decubitus with effusion side dependent (fluid pools inferiorly)
sterileSetup: Chlorhexidine prep ×3 passes over posterior chest; allow 2 minutes to dry
Large sterile drape, fenestrated over target site
Sterile gloves and mask
Mayo stand in order: lidocaine syringe, exploring needle, thoracentesis catheter, collection tubing, drainage bag
Drainage collection bag gravity-dependent and below patient level before aspiration begins
backupEquipment: Pigtail catheter (14–16 Fr Seldinger kit): for prolonged drainage over days, loculated effusion, or empyema not draining through needle
Chest tube (24–28 Fr): if empyema is confirmed or frank pus encountered
Needle decompression: if tension pneumothorax develops with acute hemodynamic compromise post-procedure
references: Gordon CE, Feller-Kopman D, Balk EM, Smetana GW. Pneumothorax following thoracentesis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Arch Intern Med. 2010;170(4):332–339.
Feller-Kopman DJ, et al. Management of Pleural Disease. Lancet. 2022;400(10355):1157–1168.
Light RW. Parapneumonic effusions and empyema. Proc Am Thorac Soc. 2006;3(1):75–80.
ACCP Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Lung Cancer, 3rd ed. Pleural Effusion section. 2013.

title: Pericardiocentesis
subtitle: Emergency relief of pericardial tamponade by subxiphoid or apical approach
inKit: Pericardiocentesis needle (18g spinal needle, 6–9 cm, or commercial pericardiocentesis kit)
Guidewire (0.035" J-tip) and pigtail or straight drainage catheter (if Seldinger kit)
50–60 mL syringes ×2 for aspiration
Three-way stopcock
Sterile drapes and chlorhexidine prep applicators
1–2% lidocaine, 10 mL syringe with 25g needle for skin infiltration
ECG alligator clip (V-lead monitor wire) to attach to needle hub for landmark guidance when echo unavailable
outsideKit: Bedside echocardiography (phased-array or curvilinear probe, 2–5 MHz) — pericardiocentesis without echo in a non-arrest setting is below standard of care; confirm effusion size and approach window
Sterile ultrasound probe cover for real-time echo guidance
12-lead ECG machine: V-lead alligator clip monitoring if echo is unavailable
Continuous cardiac monitor with rhythm strip throughout the procedure
Crash cart at bedside: defibrillator charged, epinephrine 1 mg and atropine 0.5 mg drawn
Cardiothoracic surgery on notice before starting: surgical pericardial window may be needed if drainage fails
Hemodynamic monitoring: continuous BP, pulse ox, waveform; IV access ×2 running
commonlyForgotten: Echocardiographic guidance before needle insertion — echo confirms effusion location, size, and approach, and avoids RV free-wall puncture
Watching continuously for ST elevation or current-of-injury on V-lead monitoring — sign that needle tip has entered the ventricle; pull back immediately
Not removing more than 50–100 mL rapidly from chronic large effusions — sudden afterload change causes RV dilation and reflex hypotension
Leaving a drainage catheter in situ (if Seldinger kit used) for 24–48 hours — tamponade recurrence within hours is common in malignant and purulent effusions
Blood that does NOT clot = pericardial hemorrhage; blood that CLOTS = you are in a cardiac chamber — this distinction is clinically critical
patientSetup: Head of bed elevated 30–45° — gravity pools effusion inferiorly and anteriorly, improving subxiphoid window
Confirm approach window on echo BEFORE prepping: subxiphoid vs. apical is determined by where the largest effusion pocket is with least lung interposition
Subxiphoid approach: needle aimed at 45° toward left shoulder, just inferior to xiphoid process
Apical approach: 5th–6th ICS just lateral to left cardiac border, needle angled 30° posteriorly and superiorly
Volume resuscitation: IV fluid bolus before drainage if hemodynamically compromised — supports RV filling during decompression
Operator on patient's right for subxiphoid; on patient's left for apical approach
sterileSetup: Chlorhexidine prep over subxiphoid area or apical chest; allow 2 min to dry
Sterile drapes
Sterile gloves, gown, and mask
60 mL syringe attached to stopcock on needle hub before insertion
V-lead alligator clip attached to metal needle hub if using ECG monitoring method
backupEquipment: Cardiothoracic surgery for subxiphoid surgical pericardial window: failed bedside drainage, loculated effusion, trauma with hemopericardium
Pericardiocentesis under fluoroscopy (cardiac cath lab): for difficult anatomy or large loculated effusion
Balloon pericardiotomy (IR): for recurrent malignant effusions requiring definitive window without open surgery
references: Maisch B, Seferovic PM, Ristic AD, et al. Guidelines on the diagnosis and management of pericardial diseases. Eur Heart J. 2004;25(7):587–610.
Tsang TS, El-Najdawi EK, Seward JB, et al. Percutaneous echocardiographically guided pericardiocentesis in pediatric patients. Mayo Clin Proc. 1998;73(7):647–652.
Fitch MT, Nicks BA, Pariyadath M, McGinnis HD, Manthey DE. Emergency pericardiocentesis. N Engl J Med. 2012;366:e17.
